perturbation.original_text: If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within thirty (30) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Disclosing Party to present or mail the written form of orally disclosed confidential information within 15 days. Modifying this to 30 days introduces a contradiction with California's laws regarding trade secret protection, which prioritizes rapid safeguarding of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret to qualify for protection. Delaying the written formalization and communication of orally disclosed confidential information to 30 days could be deemed an unreasonable delay, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status under California law. While the statute doesn't specify a precise timeframe, the emphasis is on prompt action. Courts often consider the speed and diligence of actions taken to protect trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the timeframe for formalizing orally disclosed confidential information from 15 to 30 days. The Law Explanation states that California law requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret' and that delaying formalization 'could be deemed an unreasonable delay, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status'. While snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426, simply cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, the Law Explanation builds upon this by stating the 'emphasis is on prompt action'. The modification, by doubling the timeframe, introduces a contradiction by potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard for maintaining trade secret protection under California law.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Paragraph "4" hereof, the obligation of the Receiving Party provided in Paragraph "2" hereof shall continue for three (3) years from the date of each receipt of the Confidential Information, even after termination of this Agreement according to paragraph "3" hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Paragraph "4" hereof, the obligation of the Receiving Party provided in Paragraph "2" hereof shall continue for one (1) year from the date of each receipt of the Confidential Information, even after termination of this Agreement according to paragraph "3" hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that confidentiality obligations last for three years. Reducing this to one year could be problematic under California law, where the duration of trade secret protection can extend beyond one year if the information retains its value and secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets with a longer lifespan of value, potentially conflicting with the intended protection afforded by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality obligation from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this could conflict with California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year limit might be insufficient if the trade secret's value extends beyond that period, creating a contradiction. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code section 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The changed text setting a one-year limit directly undermines the potentially longer protection period afforded by trade secret law, establishing a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Japan.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Japanese law governs the agreement. Changing this to Delaware law introduces a potential conflict because Hybrid Networks, Inc. has its principal place of business in Cupertino, California. If California has a materially greater interest than Delaware in the determination of the particular issue and California law would be applied in the absence of an effective choice of law provision, this modification may be unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Choice of Law Rules (Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 410.10)
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure section 410.10 gives California courts jurisdiction over causes of action arising in California. A choice-of-law provision specifying Delaware law may not be enforced if California has a materially greater interest in the determination of the particular issue. This is especially true if applying Delaware law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.10 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The change from Japanese law to Delaware law directly contradicts the principle that the governing law should be chosen based on the location of the parties and the interests of the state. According to the provided explanation, if California has a materially greater interest in the determination of the particular issue and California law would be applied in the absence of an effective choice of law provision, this modification may be unenforceable under California law, creating a legal issue. The Law Explanation confirms that California Code of Civil Procedure section 410.10 gives California courts jurisdiction over causes of action arising in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this by stating that 'A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States,' implying that California courts can assert jurisdiction if the cause of action arises there, potentially overriding the Delaware choice of law.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned or delivered to the Disclosing Party, with all copies made thereof, in whatever form, if the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party may be retained by the Receiving Party at its discretion, regardless of whether the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the return of confidential information upon request. Changing this to allow the Receiving Party to retain the information at its discretion contradicts the principle of trade secret protection under California law, where reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy, including controlling access and preventing unauthorized use or disclosure. It also goes against common law for contractual confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California law, failing to return confidential information upon request demonstrates a lack of reasonable effort to maintain its secrecy, potentially undermining trade secret protection and giving rise to claims of misappropriation. This change directly contradicts the requirement to actively protect and control access to confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of protecting confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The original text mandates the return of confidential information upon request, aligning with the need to protect trade secrets. The changed text allows the receiving party to retain confidential information at its discretion, regardless of the disclosing party's request. This directly conflicts with California Civil Code 3426, which, as cited in Scraped Law Snippet 1, is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Allowing the receiving party to retain confidential information, even after a request for its return, demonstrates a lack of reasonable effort to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information 2 <PAGE> and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to any individual or entity at its discretion, without regard to whether they have a need to know the information or are obligated under any confidentiality terms.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits disclosure to those with a 'need to know' and bound by confidentiality. Removing these restrictions and allowing disclosure to anyone contradicts the fundamental principle of maintaining trade secret status under California law, which requires reasonable efforts to prevent unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of trade secrets. This change eliminates any control over disclosure, making it practically impossible to maintain secrecy and jeopardizing trade secret protection. Unfettered disclosure, as permitted by the modified text, would constitute a failure to take reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the requirement to protect trade secrets under California law. The original text restricts disclosure to those with a 'need to know' and bound by confidentiality agreements. The changed text allows disclosure to 'any individual or entity at its discretion' without these restrictions. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this change eliminates any control over disclosure, and the Law Explanation states it makes it practically impossible to maintain secrecy and jeopardizes trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), confirms the existence of this body of law. Allowing unfettered disclosure directly undermines the reasonable measures needed to protect trade secrets, a foundational principle for maintaining their legal status.
